title: ElementsLearningII
markdown:
## The Elements of Statistical Learning

**Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction — Second Edition**

*Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani, Jerome Friedman*
Stanford University | Springer Series in Statistics

---

## Overview

During the past decade there has been an explosion in computation and information technology. With it have come vast amounts of data in a variety of fields such as medicine, biology, finance, and marketing. The challenge of understanding these data has led to the development of new tools in the field of statistics, and spawned new areas such as **data mining**, **machine learning**, and **bioinformatics**. Many of these tools have common underpinnings but are often expressed with different terminology.

This book describes the important ideas in these areas in a common conceptual framework. While the approach is statistical, the emphasis is on concepts rather than mathematics. The book's coverage is broad, from **supervised learning** (prediction) to **unsupervised learning**.

### Topics Covered

- Neural networks
- Support vector machines
- Classification trees and boosting
- Graphical models
- Random forests
- Ensemble methods
- Least angle regression & path algorithms for the lasso
- Non-negative matrix factorization
- Spectral clustering
- Methods for "wide" data ($p \gg N$), including multiple testing and false discovery rates

### About the Authors

- **Trevor Hastie** — Co-developed generalized additive models; co-developed much of the statistical modeling software in R/S-PLUS; invented principal curves and surfaces.
- **Robert Tibshirani** — Proposed the **lasso**; co-author of *An Introduction to the Bootstrap*.
- **Jerome Friedman** — Co-inventor of **CART**, **MARS**, **projection pursuit**, and **gradient boosting**.

---

## Preface to the Second Edition

> *"In God we trust, all others bring data."*
> — William Edwards Deming (1900–1993)

### What's New in Each Chapter

| Chapter | What's New |
|---|---|
| 3. Linear Methods for Regression | LAR algorithm and generalizations of the lasso |
| 4. Linear Methods for Classification | Lasso path for logistic regression |
| 5. Basis Expansions and Regularization | Additional illustrations of RKHS |
| 7. Model Assessment and Selection | Strengths and pitfalls of cross-validation |
| 10. Boosting and Additive Trees | New ecology example; material split off to Ch. 16 |
| 11. Neural Networks | Bayesian neural nets and the NIPS 2003 challenge |
| 12. Support Vector Machines and Flexible Discriminants | Path algorithm for SVM classifier |
| 14. Unsupervised Learning | Spectral clustering, kernel PCA, sparse PCA, non-negative matrix factorization, archetypal analysis, nonlinear dimension reduction, Google PageRank, direct approach to ICA |
| **15. Random Forests** | **New chapter** |
| **16. Ensemble Learning** | **New chapter** |
| **17. Undirected Graphical Models** | **New chapter** |
| **18. High-Dimensional Problems** | **New chapter** |

### Additional Notes

- Color palette changed from red/green to **orange/blue** contrast for colorblind accessibility.
- Chapter 6 renamed from "Kernel Methods" to **"Kernel Smoothing Methods"** to avoid confusion with SVM kernel methods.
- Error-rate estimation in Chapter 7 now clearly differentiates **conditional error rates** (conditional on training set) from **unconditional rates**.
- Chapters 15 and 16 follow naturally from Chapter 10 and are best read in that order.
- Chapter 17 covers only **undirected graphical models**; directed graphical models are omitted.
- Chapter 18 explores the $p \gg N$ problem — learning in high-dimensional feature spaces (genomics, proteomics, document classification).

---

## Preface to the First Edition

> *"We are drowning in information and starving for knowledge."*
> — Rutherford D. Roger

The field of statistics is constantly challenged by the problems that science and industry bring to its door. With the advent of computers and the information age, statistical problems have exploded both in size and complexity. Challenges in data storage, organization, and searching have led to the new field of "data mining"; statistical and computational problems in biology and medicine have created "bioinformatics."

> **Key distinction:** The learning problems considered can be roughly categorized as either **supervised** or **unsupervised**. In supervised learning, the goal is to predict the value of an outcome measure based on input measures; in unsupervised learning, there is no outcome measure, and the goal is to describe associations and patterns among inputs.

> *"There is no true interpretation of anything; interpretation is a vehicle in the service of human comprehension. The value of interpretation is in enabling others to fruitfully think about an idea."*
> — Andreas Buja

---

## Contents

### Part I — Foundations

1. **Introduction**
2. **Overview of Supervised Learning**
   - Variable types and terminology
   - Least squares and nearest neighbors
   - Statistical decision theory
   - Local methods in high dimensions
   - Statistical models and function approximation
   - Structured regression models
   - Classes of restricted estimators
   - Model selection and the bias–variance tradeoff

### Part II — Linear Methods

3. **Linear Methods for Regression**
   - Linear regression models and least squares
   - The Gauss–Markov theorem
   - Subset selection (best-subset, forward/backward stepwise, forward stagewise)
   - Shrinkage methods: **ridge regression**, **the lasso**, **least angle regression (LAR)**
   - Principal components regression and partial least squares
   - The Dantzig selector, grouped lasso, pathwise coordinate optimization

4. **Linear Methods for Classification**
   - Linear regression of an indicator matrix
   - **Linear discriminant analysis** (LDA) and regularized/reduced-rank variants
   - **Logistic regression** — fitting, inference, $L_1$ regularization
   - Separating hyperplanes — Rosenblatt's perceptron, optimal separating hyperplanes

### Part III — Nonlinear Methods

5. **Basis Expansions and Regularization**
   - Piecewise polynomials and splines (natural cubic splines)
   - Smoothing splines and automatic parameter selection
   - Nonparametric logistic regression
   - Multidimensional splines
   - **Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces (RKHS)**
   - **Wavelet smoothing** — wavelet bases, transform, and adaptive filtering

6. **Kernel Smoothing Methods**
   - One-dimensional kernel smoothers
   - Local linear and local polynomial regression
   - Kernel width selection
   - Structured local regression in $\mathbb{R}^p$
   - Local likelihood and other models
   - Kernel density estimation and classification

### Part IV — Model Assessment and Inference

7. **Model Assessment and Selection**
8. **Model Inference and Averaging**

### Part V — Flexible Methods

9. **Additive Models, Trees, and Related Methods**
10. **Boosting and Additive Trees**
11. **Neural Networks**
12. **Support Vector Machines and Flexible Discriminants**
13. **Prototype Methods and Nearest-Neighbors**

### Part VI — Unsupervised and Advanced

14. **Unsupervised Learning**
15. **Random Forests** *(new)*
16. **Ensemble Learning** *(new)*
17. **Undirected Graphical Models** *(new)*
18. **High-Dimensional Problems** ($p \gg N$) *(new)*

---

> *"The quiet statisticians have changed our world; not by discovering new facts or technical developments, but by changing the ways that we reason, experiment and form our opinions."*
> — Ian Hacking
